_rationale: index_stats with no arguments — default-arg dispatch path. Iter-7 audit: this descriptor was FAILING on main with the original 'stable: text' tier (status:indexing vs status:ready single-shot mismatch — the parity runner sends ONE tool/call frame, Go's async indexer is still warming, C++ indexes synchronously before MCP loop starts at src/cli/mcp.cpp:593 so reads ready immediately). Probe: /tmp/parity-iter4/index_stats___.{go,cpp}.json. The wait-ready.parity.json descriptor was added iter-4 to lock the multi-call ready-state shape; this basic.parity.json now locks the default-arg dispatch ENVELOPE only — text body diverges by timing and is ignored. After iter-6 (DGeclu4miU5q) parity-compat-stub removal both sides reach the real handler — but the single-shot timing residue remains. Tighten by either (a) runner-level multi-call wait, (b) C++ adopting Go's async indexer, or (c) Go adopting C++ sync indexing.

tiers._rationale_stable: Envelope only. Locks content[].type='text' frame and tool dispatch (no stub shadow after iter-6 fix). Tighter text-body parity blocked on async-vs-sync timing — see wait-ready.parity.json for the multi-call workaround that achieves text-body lockdown.
tiers._rationale_ignore: result.content[].text varies between status:indexing (Go async, single-shot) and status:ready (C++ sync). NOT a silent fallback — wait-ready.parity.json holds the tighter assertion and karpathy-rule-6 is satisfied by the timing-mismatch documentation rather than a normalizer that would hide a real bug.
